author: @494demigodd
text: Stick with Bambu, don't waste your time.  Very difficult to get to play nice.  Wifi set up isn't as easy as he says because the slicer software on your computer will still show Qidi as being offline.  Difficult to firmware update, Bambu handles the setup much better and super simple.  The software user interface is just so uncomfortable.

author: @OZtwo
text: I have a QIDI X-Plus and loving it. Been waiting to update to the x-max 3 so I am able to print larger prints. The only thing holding me back are some of the issues QIDI has been having. What if any issues have you had with this printer?

author: @mwoods269
text:
My first 3d printer and I must say, nothing in the instructions or videos like this tell you to put the "build plate" on before running the setup. If you don't put that metallic plate on, the bed will slam into the hot end repeatedly. Ruined my first QIDI X Max 3. The replacement came, I followed the directions, didn't install that plate and watched it attempt to hit the hotend again and unplugged it. 

After inspection I noticed what looks like a proximity sensor on the side of the hotend so I figured that plate might be needed for reflection etc. That was it. Luckily no damage this time.

Am I just missing this step in the instructions? I mean after thinking about it you would need that thickness on there to calibrate but it just didn't hit me until further troubleshooting.

author: @MrDoyley35
text:
One thing I have wondered about was wether it would be possible with conductive printable plastics of differing electrical conductivity to 3D print a thermoelectric mesh circuit. Wearable thermoelectric items such as a cycling helmet that could power an led would be quite literally cool and also useful. 
Not sure if it would be possible with the current filament technology however. I suppose one could sew and solder some wires into the helmet to achieve the same thing.

author: @rtfazeberdee3519

author: @JBracale128
text:
Hey man! Your electric oven build was so inspiring i'm trying to build my own for ceramics!
I need some help calculating the size and how many coils i'll need.
I got a 50l barrel and insulated it with firebrick and ceramic wool leaving me with a inner chamber like a cyllinder of 22cm diameter and 24cm deep.
What size wire and how much would i need to get to 1200c with that inner chamber size? Any help would be awesome thanks!
Love from Brasil !!

author: @smellslikeupdog80
text:
Hey dude, I see you've gotten into 3d printing in recent years.
I remember you from how you made graphene inks from grafoil.  
I replicated mixing some of your DIY graphene with ABS plastic.  I worked in injection molding at the time, and It was pretty tough plastic.  You also made some bioplastic at the time.

Have you ever considered making or getting your hands on a small scale extruder to mix up some different batches of a more modern graphene 3d printing filament?  I've looked at the ones from china to make the weedwhacker filament style, but I'm also considering using a small scale injection molding machine to experiment with multi-plstics composits as pull-trusion might let me get away with higher graphene pigment loading.

Wanted to check in if you had scratched that yet, because loads of stuff would be neat with conductive filament -- including electroplating over the plastic, RF shielding, self-lubricating gears & bearings....  Some bloke made a decent paper on semi-industrial scale graphene with cap discharged in an argon enviroment with recirculating graphite powders last year; also have yet to try it.

too much to do, too little time.  Cheers mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's only in the last year mate

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Thank you for the great review. Note: A machine that large and advanced should really have 2 heads (dual extruder design) that run at the same time: one to print the part, and the other to concurrently print the support material so that 3d prints can have high quality overhangs and bridges without falling apart.
